mean, we like benchmarks to measure our progress against.

I saw you left some comments on there and I did respond to them in Airtable.
I think you can think of it, like a lot of it's a question of the difficulty is time.

You know, and one of the reasons why we like to sprinkle in the lower and higher and the difficulty in volume is because if you have a keyword that's high volume and high difficulty, it's just going to take longer to rank for.
But I think, you know, you have you're like have a good authority.

It's a it's a topic that if it's a topic that's in your space and if we create high quality content and I think the key here is differentiated content, you know, not just kind of regurgitating what's out there, but making sure it's content that brings sort of.
Cresta's specific positioning and kind of unique point of view there, it will rank.

It'll just take longer, you know, you're talking, it'll slowly make its way up, but it could take six months to get on to like that top ranking position, which is why you wouldn't want to do only those keywords because then you're sitting around waiting.
But it's also good to do some of them early because, you know, the clock starts when you publish it.

So I would say just thinking about it in terms of balance, like there's not a reason not to go after those, especially if because they're often like really high value keywords, which is what makes it difficult.
We just wouldn't want to only publish those for, you know, a month.
Yep, makes sense.
That's helpful.
And then what about the AEO side of it?
Like, is there any sort of framework I can use to bring that in?
Yeah.
I mean, I think the reality is that a lot of...
It is similar.
For things that are high volume and high difficulty, it means people are searching them a lot.
So a lot of the same framework applies.
I think the difference for AEO is that there's a lot of opportunity for those long tail keywords.
So I think sometimes keywords that have really low volume because they're like super specific play really well in AEO because they're often also really high intent.
Like it would be someone who's like looking for a specific feature or like much closer to the point of purchase, like evaluating something really specific.

So I think when you look at them, if there's something that feels like it has low volume, but is kind of in your sales funnel is kind of closer to that point of purchase, it can often be a good one to go after because that the traffic that comes from LL.
Tends to be really high intent, like even more than search.
So all to say, I think one thing is like not to discount those lower volume or long tail keywords, because I think they can be really good for AEO.
I think things like lists, like there's a ton of citations going to lists right now.
So those comparison articles, those like top 10 articles, they are just like far and away the most common type of content that's being cited.
So I think one reason is because, you know, the way AI search works is it kind of like chunks the content and like a top 10 list has a nice, like good chunk for AI search to serve someone.
So I think that's part of it.
So some of it, I think, is when we approach those kind of lists, making sure that we're...

Striking a balance between having it be useful educational content, but making sure we're kind of getting your product mentions in those chunks, like your brand mentions in those chunks, so that it doesn't just get cited.
It also kind of feeds into that brand recognition, so that you're actually showing up in those answers as well.
So I think some of it's the approach is a little bit different.
And so I think those are some of the guidelines, but overall, I think they're very similar.
Like if something is important to your brand and high priority in terms of search, it's going to be as well for AEO.
